pain_point_label: Chronic Absenteeism & Dropout Without Early Intervention

matches.match_rationale: Chronic absenteeism is fundamentally an anomaly detection problem — students whose attendance patterns deviate from expected behavior need to be flagged before the pattern becomes a crisis. A multi-site operator with 10-15 campuses needs automated detection of abnormal absence streaks, not manual review. This directly addresses the 'patterns of absence not flagged until crisis' mechanism.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An anomaly detection layer over the SIS/attendance system that flags students whose absence frequency, clustering, or trajectory deviates significantly from cohort baselines — surfaced as a daily alert dashboard for each site coordinator.

matches.match_rationale: Dropout risk prediction is a canonical propensity modeling problem — given student features (attendance trend, grade trajectory, demographics, engagement signals), predict the probability that a specific student will disenroll within 30-60 days. This directly addresses the 'dropout risk not predicted early' and 'tuition-bearing students disappearing without follow-up' mechanisms. Education has well-established labeled datasets for this.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A dropout propensity model trained on historical enrollment, attendance, and academic data that scores every active student weekly and routes high-risk students to a counselor or outreach queue, prioritized by tuition-bearing status.

matches.match_rationale: The 'no systematic parent outreach' and manual cross-site tracking burden are workflow automation problems, not just prediction problems. An autonomous agent can trigger outreach sequences (SMS, email, staff task creation) when a student crosses an absence threshold, eliminating the manual coordination across 10-15 sites. This directly addresses administrative staff spending time tracking rather than intervening.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An outreach automation agent that monitors attendance feeds, detects threshold breaches, drafts and sends templated parent notifications, logs contact attempts, and escalates to a staff follow-up task if no response is received within 48 hours.

matches.match_rationale: Parent outreach communications need to be personalized, context-sensitive, and appropriately toned — a generic SMS blast is less effective than a message that references the student's name, number of absences, and a specific call to action. Text generation enables at-scale personalized outreach without staff drafting each message manually, supporting the 'no systematic parent outreach' gap.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An LLM-powered message composer that generates personalized parent/guardian outreach messages (SMS, email) using student-specific absence data, adjusting tone based on severity level and prior contact history.

matches.match_rationale: Before propensity modeling, students can be classified into risk tiers (low/medium/high/critical) using structured attendance and engagement features — this is simpler to implement and explain to staff than a continuous score model. Multi-site operators can apply different intervention protocols by tier without needing staff to interpret raw probabilities.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A risk-tier classification model that segments every student into four intervention bands each week based on attendance rate, absence streaks, and prior-term patterns, feeding directly into site-level coordination workflows.
